name: Cain Kills Abel
description: The first murder in human history. Cain, jealous that God accepted Abel's offering but rejected his own, rose up against his brother and killed him in the field.

name: War of the Nine Kings
description: A coalition of four eastern kings defeated five kings of the Jordan plain in the Valley of Siddim. The victors plundered Sodom and Gomorrah and took captives, including Lot, Abraham's nephew.
belligerents: Chedorlaomer of Elam, Amraphel of Shinar, Arioch of Ellasar, Tidal of Goiim
Bera of Sodom, Birsha of Gomorrah, Shinab of Admah, Shemeber of Zeboiim, King of Bela/Zoar

name: Abraham Rescues Lot
description: Abraham mustered 318 trained men from his household, pursued the four kings to Dan, launched a night attack, and routed them as far as Hobah north of Damascus. He recovered all the people and goods, including Lot.

name: Destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah
description: God rained sulfur and fire from heaven upon Sodom, Gomorrah, and the surrounding cities, utterly destroying them for their wickedness. Only Lot and his two daughters escaped, as his wife looked back and became a pillar of salt.

name: Jacob Wrestles with God
description: On the night before meeting Esau, Jacob wrestled with a mysterious figure until daybreak. The figure touched Jacob's hip socket, dislocating it, but Jacob refused to let go until he received a blessing. He was renamed Israel — 'he who strives with God.'

name: Massacre at Shechem
description: After Shechem son of Hamor violated their sister Dinah, Simeon and Levi deceived the Shechemites into circumcising themselves, then attacked and killed every male while they were incapacitated. They plundered the city and took the women and children.

name: Ten Plagues of Egypt
description: God sent ten devastating plagues upon Egypt — blood, frogs, gnats, flies, livestock pestilence, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and death of the firstborn — to break Pharaoh's will and liberate Israel from slavery. The final plague, Passover, became Israel's defining act of redemption.

name: Destruction of Pharaoh's Army at the Red Sea
description: Pharaoh pursued Israel with six hundred chosen chariots and his entire mounted force. God parted the Red Sea, allowing Israel to cross on dry ground between walls of water. When the Egyptians followed, God threw their army into confusion, clogging chariot wheels. At dawn He released the waters, drowning every soldier. Not one survived.

name: Battle of Rephidim
description: The Amalekites attacked Israel's rear, targeting the weak and weary. Joshua led the fight while Moses held up his hands on the hill — when his arms dropped, Amalek prevailed. Aaron and Hur held his arms up until sunset, securing victory.

name: Defeat at Hormah
description: After the faithless spy report, God condemned the exodus generation to die in the wilderness. The Israelites then presumed to invade Canaan without God's blessing or the Ark. The Amalekites and Canaanites routed them and drove them back to Hormah.

name: Victory at Hormah
description: When the Canaanite king of Arad attacked Israel and took captives, Israel vowed to the Lord and God delivered the Canaanites. Israel utterly destroyed their cities, and the place was named Hormah, meaning 'destruction.'

name: Defeat of Sihon King of the Amorites
description: Israel requested peaceful passage through Amorite territory, but Sihon refused and attacked. Israel defeated him at Jahaz and seized all his territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok, including his capital Heshbon.

name: Defeat of Og King of Bashan
description: Og, the giant king of Bashan whose iron bed was nine cubits long, marched out against Israel at Edrei. God told Moses not to fear him. Israel struck him down with his sons and his entire army, leaving no survivors, and took possession of Bashan.

name: War Against Midian
description: God commanded vengeance against Midian for seducing Israel into idolatry and sexual immorality at Baal-Peor. Twelve thousand Israelite soldiers killed the five Midianite kings and Balaam the prophet, destroyed their cities, and took enormous spoil.

name: Battle of Jericho
description: Israel marched around Jericho once daily for six days in eerie silence, then seven times on the seventh day. The priests blew rams' horn trumpets, the people raised a deafening war cry, and the massive walls collapsed outward. Israel devoted the city to total destruction; only Rahab the prostitute and her family survived, sheltered because she had hidden Israel's spies.

name: First Battle of Ai — Defeat
description: Confident after Jericho, Israel sent only 3,000 men against Ai. The men of Ai routed them, killing about 36 Israelites. The cause was Achan's theft of devoted things from Jericho — hidden sin in the camp broke the covenant.

name: Second Battle of Ai — Victory
description: After dealing with Achan's sin, Joshua employed an ambush strategy. He feigned retreat to draw Ai's defenders out, then a hidden force of 5,000 seized and burned the city. The men of Ai were caught between the two Israelite forces and annihilated.

name: Battle of Gibeon — The Sun Stands Still
description: Five Amorite kings besieged Gibeon for making peace with Israel. Joshua marched all night from Gilgal and caught them off guard at dawn. God hurled enormous hailstones from heaven that killed more than the sword. Then Joshua commanded the sun to stand still over Gibeon and the moon over the Valley of Aijalon, and daylight held for nearly a full day so Israel could complete the rout. There has never been a day like it before or since.
belligerents: Amorite coalition — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon

name: Joshua's Southern Campaign
description: Following the victory at Gibeon, Joshua swept through the southern lowlands in a rapid campaign. He captured and destroyed Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish (defeating Gezer's relief force), Eglon, Hebron, and Debir. He executed the five imprisoned Amorite kings at Makkedah.
belligerents: Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish, Gezer, Eglon, Hebron, Debir

name: Battle at the Waters of Merom
description: Jabin of Hazor assembled the largest coalition against Israel — forces 'as many as the sand on the seashore' with horses and chariots. God told Joshua not to fear. Israel attacked suddenly, routed the coalition, hamstrung their horses, and burned their chariots. Joshua then burned Hazor itself.
belligerents: Northern Canaanite coalition — Hazor, Madon, Shimron, Achshaph, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Jebusites, Hivites

name: Conquest Summary — Thirty-One Kings Defeated
description: Joshua 12 lists all thirty-one kings defeated during the Conquest: Jericho, Ai, Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon, Gezer, Debir, Geder, Hormah, Arad, Libnah, Adullam, Makkedah, Bethel, Tappuah, Hepher, Aphek, Lasharon, Madon, Hazor, Shimron-meron, Achshaph, Taanach, Megiddo, Kedesh, Jokneam, Dor, Goiim, and Tirzah.

name: Othniel Delivers Israel
description: After eight years of oppression under Cushan-Rishathaim of Mesopotamia, the Spirit of the Lord came upon Othniel, Caleb's nephew. He judged Israel and went to war, and God delivered the enemy into his hands. The land had rest for forty years.

name: Ehud's Assassination of Eglon and Defeat of Moab
description: After eighteen years of Moabite oppression, the left-handed Benjamite Ehud concealed a short sword and gained a private audience with the obese King Eglon. He assassinated Eglon, escaped, rallied Israel, and seized the Jordan fords. Israel struck down about 10,000 Moabite warriors.

name: Shamgar vs. the Philistines
description: Shamgar struck down six hundred Philistines with an ox goad — a wooden stick tipped with metal used for driving cattle. With this single feat, he delivered Israel.

name: Deborah and Barak vs. Sisera and the Canaanites
description: Jabin's commander Sisera had 900 iron chariots and had oppressed Israel for twenty years. Deborah summoned Barak to Mount Tabor with 10,000 men. God sent a rainstorm that bogged down Sisera's chariots in the Kishon River. Sisera fled on foot and was killed by Jael, who drove a tent peg through his skull.

name: Gideon vs. the Midianites
description: God reduced Gideon's army from 32,000 to just 300 men so that Israel could not boast in its own strength. At night, the 300 surrounded the vast Midianite camp with trumpets, torches hidden inside clay jars, and a battle cry: 'A sword for the Lord and for Gideon!' When the jars shattered and the trumpets blared, God turned the Midianites against each other in blind panic. The rout was total, and Israel pursued the fleeing remnant across the Jordan.

name: Abimelech's Destruction of Shechem
description: Gideon's son Abimelech seized power by murdering his 70 brothers. When Shechem revolted, he razed the city, sowed it with salt, and burned 1,000 people alive in the tower of Shechem. He was later killed at Thebez when a woman dropped a millstone on his head.

name: Siege of Thebez — Death of Abimelech
description: Abimelech besieged Thebez after destroying Shechem. As he approached the tower to burn it, a woman dropped an upper millstone on his head, cracking his skull. He ordered his armor-bearer to kill him so no one could say a woman slew him.

name: Philistines Capture the Ark — Battle of Aphek
description: Israel fought the Philistines near Aphek and lost 4,000 men. They fetched the Ark of the Covenant as a talisman, but God was not with them. The Philistines routed Israel, killed 30,000 foot soldiers including Hophni and Phinehas, and captured the Ark. When Eli heard the news, he fell backward, broke his neck, and died.

name: Jephthah vs. the Ammonites
description: The outcast Jephthah was recalled to lead Gilead against Ammon. After failed diplomacy, the Spirit of the Lord came upon him and he struck the Ammonites from Aroer to Minnith — twenty cities — with a very great slaughter. He had made a rash vow before the battle that cost him dearly.

name: War Against Benjamin — Battle of Gibeah
description: After the horrific crime at Gibeah — the gang rape and murder of a Levite's concubine — and Benjamin's refusal to hand over the perpetrators, Israel went to war against its own tribe. Israel lost 40,000 men in two defeats before God granted victory on the third day. Benjamin was nearly annihilated: only 600 men survived.

name: Jephthah vs. Ephraim — Shibboleth
description: Ephraim threatened Jephthah for not summoning them to fight Ammon. Civil war erupted. Gilead seized the Jordan fords and identified fleeing Ephraimites by asking them to say 'Shibboleth' — they could only pronounce 'Sibboleth.' Forty-two thousand Ephraimites died.

name: Samuel's Victory at Mizpah
description: Samuel gathered all Israel at Mizpah for repentance and prayer. The Philistines attacked during the assembly, but God thundered with a great voice against them, throwing them into confusion. Israel pursued and struck them down. Samuel raised the Ebenezer stone — 'thus far the Lord has helped us.'

name: Samson Kills the Lion
description: On his way to Timnah to court a Philistine woman, the Spirit of the Lord rushed upon Samson and he tore a young lion apart with his bare hands as if it were a young goat. He told no one.

name: Samson Slays Thirty at Ashkelon
description: When the Philistines solved his riddle through his wife's betrayal, the Spirit of the Lord rushed upon Samson. He went to Ashkelon, struck down thirty men, took their garments to pay the wager, and returned home burning with anger.

name: Samson Burns the Philistine Fields
description: After learning his wife had been given to another man, Samson caught 300 foxes, tied torches between their tails in pairs, and released them into the Philistine standing grain, vineyards, and olive groves, destroying their harvest.

name: Samson at Lehi — Jawbone of a Donkey
description: The Philistines came to capture Samson. The men of Judah bound him and delivered him to the enemy. The Spirit of the Lord rushed upon him, the ropes melted off his arms, he seized a fresh jawbone of a donkey and struck down 1,000 men.

name: Samson Carries the Gates of Gaza
description: The Philistines surrounded Gaza to ambush Samson. At midnight he rose, tore the city gates from their posts — doors, posts, bar and all — hoisted them on his shoulders, and carried them to the top of a hill facing Hebron, nearly 40 miles away.

name: Samson Destroys the Temple of Dagon
description: Betrayed by Delilah, blinded, and chained, Samson was brought to the temple of Dagon for the Philistines' amusement. He prayed for strength one last time, pushed apart the central pillars, and collapsed the temple on himself and about 3,000 Philistines. He killed more in his death than in his life.

name: Saul's Victory at Jabesh-gilead
description: Nahash besieged Jabesh-gilead and offered to gouge out every man's right eye as terms of surrender. The Spirit of God rushed upon Saul; he cut a yoke of oxen in pieces and sent them throughout Israel as a call to arms. He mustered 330,000 men, launched a night attack in three companies, and destroyed the Ammonites by noon.

name: Jonathan at Michmash Pass
description: Jonathan and his armor-bearer secretly climbed the steep pass between Bozez and Seneh to attack the Philistine garrison. They killed twenty men in an area of about half an acre. God sent an earthquake and panic. The Philistines turned on each other, and Israel routed them.

name: Saul's Campaign Against the Amalekites
description: God commanded Saul to utterly destroy the Amalekites — every person, animal, and possession. Saul won the military victory but spared King Agag and the best of the livestock. Samuel declared 'to obey is better than sacrifice' and tore the kingdom from Saul. Samuel then executed Agag himself.

name: David vs. Goliath — Valley of Elah
description: For forty days, the giant Goliath — over nine feet tall in full bronze armor — defied the armies of the living God, demanding single combat while all Israel trembled. The shepherd boy David, armed only with a sling, five smooth stones, and unshakeable faith, ran toward the giant. A single stone sank into Goliath's forehead. David drew the giant's own sword and cut off his head. The Philistine army broke and fled.

name: David Saves Keilah
description: While on the run from Saul, David learned the Philistines were raiding Keilah's threshing floors. He inquired of God, then attacked and struck down the Philistines with a great blow, saving the city's grain supply and inhabitants.

name: David's Philistine Raids While Serving Saul
description: While leading Saul's forces, David won every engagement. He struck the Philistines repeatedly, including a major victory where he brought back their livestock. Later, while exiled at Ziklag under Achish, David raided Israel's southern enemies while claiming to raid Judah.

name: David's Recovery of Ziklag
description: While David was away, the Amalekites raided Ziklag, burned the city, and captured all the women and children — including David's two wives. David's men spoke of stoning him. He strengthened himself in the Lord, pursued, and recovered everything. Not one person or possession was lost.

name: Battle of Mount Gilboa — Death of Saul
description: The Philistines overwhelmed Israel on Mount Gilboa. Jonathan and two of his brothers were killed. Saul was badly wounded by archers and fell on his own sword rather than be captured. The Philistines hung the bodies on the wall of Beth-shan. David's lament for Saul and Jonathan is among the most poignant in Scripture.

name: Battle at the Pool of Gibeon — David vs. Ish-bosheth
description: Abner and Joab met at the pool of Gibeon. Twelve champions from each side fought to the death simultaneously — all twenty-four fell. A fierce battle erupted; David's forces won. During the rout, Abner killed Joab's brother Asahel, creating a blood feud that shaped David's reign.

name: David Captures Jerusalem
description: The Jebusites boasted that even the blind and lame could defend their fortress. David challenged his men to enter through the water shaft. Joab led the assault, captured the stronghold of Zion, and David made it his capital — the City of David.

name: Battle of Baal-perazim
description: When the Philistines heard David was king over all Israel, they came up in force to the Valley of Rephaim. David inquired of the Lord and received permission. He struck them and said, 'The Lord has broken through my enemies like a breaking flood,' naming the place Baal-perazim.

name: Second Battle of the Valley of Rephaim
description: The Philistines returned to the Valley of Rephaim. David inquired again and God changed the tactics: 'Do not go up. Circle around behind them and come at them opposite the balsam trees. When you hear the sound of marching in the treetops, move quickly — the Lord has gone out before you.' David obeyed and struck the Philistines from Geba to Gezer.

name: David's War Against Ammon and Aram
description: After Ammon humiliated David's ambassadors, they hired Aramean mercenaries. Joab fought a two-front battle and prevailed. When the Arameans regrouped under Shobach, David personally led the army and destroyed them at Helam. The Arameans made peace and became David's vassals.
belligerents: Ammon, Aram (Syria) — Hadadezer of Zobah, Aram of Beth-rehob

name: Battle of the Forest of Ephraim — Absalom's Rebellion
description: David divided his army into three columns under Joab, Abishai, and Ittai. The dense forest worked against Absalom's larger force — 'the forest devoured more that day than the sword.' Absalom's hair caught in an oak tree; Joab killed him with three javelins despite David's plea to spare his son. David's grief — 'O my son Absalom!' — is the most anguished cry in Scripture.

name: Sheba's Rebellion
description: Sheba, a Benjamite, rallied the northern tribes against David immediately after Absalom's rebellion ended. Joab besieged him at Abel-beth-maacah. A wise woman negotiated: the city threw Sheba's head over the wall, ending the rebellion without a siege.
belligerents: Sheba son of Bichri, Northern Israelite rebels

name: Kingdom Divides — Rehoboam vs. Jeroboam
description: Rehoboam rejected the elders' counsel and promised heavier burdens than Solomon. The northern tribes revolted. Rehoboam mustered 180,000 troops to reconquer the north, but the prophet Shemaiah forbade the attack: 'This thing is from me,' said the Lord.

name: Shishak's Invasion of Judah
description: Pharaoh Shishak invaded Judah with 1,200 chariots, 60,000 horsemen, and innumerable troops. He captured the fortified cities and advanced on Jerusalem. When Judah humbled itself, God granted partial deliverance — but Shishak took the treasures of the Temple and palace, including Solomon's gold shields.

name: Battle of Mount Zemaraim — Abijah vs. Jeroboam
description: Outnumbered two to one and caught in an ambush, Judah cried out to God while the priests blew trumpets. God struck the northern army. Judah killed 500,000 of Israel's picked troops — the greatest single-battle casualty figure in the Bible. Abijah captured Bethel, Jeshanah, and Ephron.

name: Asa vs. Zerah the Ethiopian
description: Zerah marched against Judah with an army of a million men and 300 chariots. Asa prayed one of Scripture's great battle prayers: 'Lord, there is no one besides You to help in the battle between the powerful and those who have no strength. Help us, O Lord our God, for we trust in You.' God struck the Ethiopians and Asa pursued them to Gerar.

name: Ahab's First Victory over Aram at Samaria
description: Ben-hadad besieged Samaria with 32 allied kings and issued escalating demands. A prophet told Ahab that God would give victory through the young officers of the provincial commanders — 232 men led the attack. Israel's surprise sortie routed the Arameans while Ben-hadad was drinking in his tent.

name: Ahab's Second Victory over Aram at Aphek
description: The Arameans returned the next year, believing Israel's God was a 'god of the hills' who could be beaten on the plains. Israel camped opposite them 'like two little flocks of goats.' A prophet declared God would prove He rules the plains too. Israel struck down 100,000 Arameans in one day; a wall at Aphek fell on 27,000 survivors.

name: Jehoshaphat's Battle — Victory by Praise
description: A vast army from beyond the Dead Sea marched on Judah. Jehoshaphat proclaimed a fast and prayed publicly. God said 'the battle is not yours but God's.' Jehoshaphat sent singers ahead of the army praising God. The Lord set ambushes among the enemy — they turned on each other and destroyed themselves. Judah spent three days gathering the spoil.

name: Battle of Ramoth-gilead — Death of Ahab
description: Ahab disguised himself while Jehoshaphat wore royal robes. The Aramean chariots initially pursued Jehoshaphat but turned away. A random arrow struck Ahab between the joints of his armor. He propped himself in his chariot all day until evening, when he died. His blood pooled in the chariot; dogs licked it up, fulfilling Elijah's prophecy.

name: Elisha and the Blinded Aramean Army
description: The king of Aram sent horses, chariots, and a great army to capture Elisha. His servant panicked, but Elisha prayed: 'Open his eyes, Lord' — and the servant saw the hills full of horses and chariots of fire. Elisha then struck the army with blindness, led them into Samaria, and fed them a feast before sending them home. The raids stopped.

name: Jehu's Coup and Slaughter
description: Anointed by Elisha's servant, Jehu drove his chariot furiously to Jezreel. He shot King Joram through the heart, pursued and mortally wounded King Ahaziah of Judah, and ordered Jezebel thrown from a window — dogs ate her body. He then had seventy sons of Ahab beheaded and destroyed all Baal worshipers in Israel.
belligerents: House of Ahab, House of Ahaziah of Judah

name: Amaziah's Victory over Edom in the Valley of Salt
description: Amaziah mustered Judah and struck down 10,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt, captured Sela (Petra), and threw 10,000 captives from the cliffs. His success led to arrogance — he challenged Israel's King Jehoash and was humiliated at Beth-shemesh.

name: Battle of Beth-shemesh — Amaziah vs. Jehoash
description: Emboldened by his Edomite victory, Amaziah challenged Israel. Jehoash replied with the parable of the thistle and the cedar. Judah was routed, Amaziah captured, and Jehoash broke down 600 feet of Jerusalem's wall and plundered the Temple treasury.

name: Fall of Samaria — Assyrian Conquest of Israel
description: After a grueling three-year siege, the Assyrians breached Samaria's defenses and captured the capital of the northern kingdom. Sargon II deported 27,290 Israelites and scattered them across the Assyrian empire — to Halah, Habor, the Gozan River, and the cities of the Medes. Foreign peoples were resettled in their land. The ten northern tribes of Israel ceased to exist as a nation, fulfilling centuries of prophetic warnings.

name: Sennacherib's Siege of Jerusalem — Angel Destroys 185,000
description: Sennacherib's army conquered 46 Judean cities and besieged Jerusalem. The Rabshakeh mocked God publicly. Hezekiah spread the threatening letter before the Lord and prayed. Isaiah prophesied Assyria's defeat. That night the angel of the Lord struck 185,000 Assyrians dead in their camp. Sennacherib withdrew and was later murdered by his own sons.

name: Death of Josiah at Megiddo
description: Pharaoh Necho marched north to aid Assyria against Babylon. Josiah, Judah's most righteous king, inexplicably intercepted him at Megiddo. Necho warned him that God had sent him on this mission. Josiah disguised himself but was struck by Egyptian archers and died. Judah mourned him deeply.

name: First Babylonian Siege of Jerusalem
description: Nebuchadnezzar besieged Jerusalem and made Jehoiakim his vassal. He took hostages from the royal family and nobility — including Daniel and his three friends — and carried sacred vessels from the Temple to Babylon.

name: Second Babylonian Siege — Deportation of Jehoiachin
description: When Jehoiakim rebelled, Nebuchadnezzar returned. Jehoiachin surrendered after three months. Nebuchadnezzar deported 10,000 people — the king, his court, warriors, craftsmen, and smiths — including the prophet Ezekiel. He installed Zedekiah as puppet king.

name: Fall of Jerusalem — Destruction of Solomon's Temple
description: After an 18-month siege during which famine became so severe mothers ate their children, the Babylonians breached the walls. Zedekiah fled but was captured near Jericho. Nebuchadnezzar killed his sons before his eyes, then blinded him. The Temple, palace, and every important building was burned. The walls were demolished. The remaining population was deported.

name: Jews Defend Themselves — Purim
description: Haman's plot to annihilate the Jews was reversed. King Ahasuerus granted the Jews the right to defend themselves. On the appointed day they killed 75,000 of their enemies and Haman's ten sons were hanged. The feast of Purim commemorates this deliverance.

name: Battle of Beth Horon — Maccabean Revolt
description: Seron, a Seleucid general, marched against the Maccabees to make a name for himself. Judas Maccabeus attacked his force in the narrow Beth Horon pass despite being vastly outnumbered. The surprise assault routed the Seleucids, killing 800 and chasing the rest to the coastal plain.

name: Battle of Emmaus
description: The Seleucids sent a massive army with slave traders following to buy Jewish captives. Gorgias took a detachment to find Judas at night, but Judas moved his camp and attacked the main Seleucid force at dawn. The enemy fled, and when Gorgias returned to find the camp burning, his force scattered too.

name: Battle of Beth Zur
description: Lysias, the Seleucid regent, invaded Judea from the south with a large force. Judas met him at Beth Zur, south of Jerusalem, and defeated the army. Lysias withdrew to Antioch to raise more troops. This opened the road to Jerusalem.

name: Rededication of the Temple — Hanukkah
description: After recapturing Jerusalem, Judas cleansed the Temple that Antiochus IV had desecrated with a pagan altar. They built a new altar, restored the sacred vessels, and rededicated the Temple on 25 Kislev. The celebration lasted eight days and became the annual Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah).

name: Massacre of the Innocents
description: When the Magi did not return with Jesus' location, Herod ordered the execution of all male children in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under. Joseph had already fled to Egypt with Mary and Jesus, warned by an angel in a dream.

name: Jesus Cleanses the Temple
description: Jesus made a whip of cords and drove out the money changers and animal sellers from the Temple courts, overturning their tables and scattering their coins. He declared, 'My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers.'

name: Arrest in Gethsemane — Peter's Sword
description: Judas led an armed band to arrest Jesus. Peter drew a sword and cut off the ear of the high priest's servant Malchus. Jesus healed Malchus and rebuked Peter: 'Put your sword away. Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?' He could have summoned twelve legions of angels but submitted willingly.
